About Me

Aerospace Engineer
& Systems Thinker

My name is Dinuka Hannadige, and I am an aerospace engineering student based in Boulder, Colorado. I am currently pursuing a Bachelor of Science in Aerospace Engineering at the University of Colorado Boulder, with a strong interest in engineering design, CAD modeling, and hands-on problem solving.

Through coursework and competitive engineering teams, I have developed experience in the engineering design process, technical documentation, and collaborative project work. Outside of academics, I serve as a propulsion engineer for Colorado Design Build Fly, motivated to apply engineering principles to real-world challenges throughout my academic career.

03

Projects

P.01
Formula SAE
ER-08 Race Car
2023 — 2024
ERAU Motorsports
Competition Day — ER-08 on Track The ERAU Motorsports ER-08 (#106) navigating an autocross course at the FSAE competition. The aerodynamic nose cone and sidepods I helped design are clearly visible, generating downforce through the high-speed corners.
Chassis Delivery The freshly-delivered ER-08 tubular steel spaceframe (foreground) arrives in the ERAU Motorsports workshop, with the previous ER-07 visible behind it. The gold bow marks the moment the team takes ownership of the new chassis.
Bare Spaceframe — Front View A detailed frontal view of the welded tubular steel spaceframe. This critical structure serves as the mounting point for the double wishbone suspension geometry and the aerodynamic package I helped develop and validate.
Aerodynamics & Vehicle Dynamics Engineer

As a key engineering team member for ERAU Motorsports competing in the global FSAE collegiate design series, I contributed to designing, building, and racing a high-performance formula-style vehicle that balanced speed, agility, and cost-effectiveness.

  • Utilized Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) tools to design specific aero components, improving downforce and cornering capabilities of the ER-08.
  • Contributed to development of an aero rake and conducted pressure tapping tests to correlate CFD models against real-world on-track performance data.
  • Tuned the double wishbone suspension system to optimize mechanical grip and handling predictability.
  • Participated through Critical Design Review (CDR) and early shakedown testing, troubleshooting mechanical integration before competition.
P.02
Colorado Design
Build Fly (AIAA)
2025 — 2026
CU Boulder
Flight Test Day — Colorado DBF Team The full Colorado Design Build Fly team gathered at the airfield following a successful prototype flight test. Field validation sessions like this are essential for tuning our aerodynamic models and building pilot confidence before competition.
Prototype — Systems Layout Top-down view of our "Pink Foam" rapid prototype. This low-fidelity model verified the placement of dual propulsion motors, ESCs, servos, and the banner-release mechanism before committing to expensive composite materials.
Pre-Flight Integration Check Collaborating with the avionics and structures leads to verify control surface mixing, servo throws, and structural integrity of the airframe immediately before a test flight at the airfield.
Mission Verification — Aircraft in Flight The prototype successfully airborne during a validation lap. For the 2026 AIAA competition, we must demonstrate sustained flight while towing a banner and carrying a full passenger and cargo payload — this flight confirmed our propulsion system meets the required thrust-to-weight ratio.
Propulsion Engineer

Core member of CU Boulder's DBF team competing in the international AIAA Design/Build/Fly competition. The 2025–2026 RFP challenged the team to design a Banner-Towing Bush Plane with STOL, banner-deploy, and payload-carrying mission requirements.

  • Conducted static thrust testing on various motor and propeller combinations, verifying performance against thrust-to-weight ratio calculations for high-drag banner-tow missions.
  • Analyzed battery discharge curves to optimize energy density for multiple flight laps while minimizing airframe weight.
  • Collaborated with Structures to design and fabricate a custom Landing Gear Test Pad for empirical durability testing against hard landings.
  • Led propulsion avionics integration into the fuselage, ensuring proper cooling, CG placement, and reliable power distribution.
  • Hands-on rapid prototyping using foam cutting and composite techniques to build a lightweight and repairable airframe.
P.03
KlisterFixer
Applicator
2025
CU Boulder GEEN 2400
Demo Day Presentation — GEEN 2400 ExpoThe full KlisterFixer team at the Engineering Projects for the Community expo, presenting the final prototype and poster to judges and peers. Left to right: Molly More, Kristina Morgunova, Aiden Bunas, Cooper Smith, and Dinuka Hannadige.
Early Indicator CircuitInitial Arduino UNO breadboard prototype testing the LED temperature-feedback logic — red and green LEDs indicate heating state before integration into the final PCB design.
Heating Element IntegrationArduino-controlled circuit driving a circular resistive heating pad alongside a green status LED, validating the thermal actuation chain and sensor-to-controller feedback loop.
Bench Validation — Lab ElectronicsFull validation setup with Agilent multimeter and Keysight programmable DC power supply, confirming the heating element's 11.3V / 1.13A / 12.8W operating point and verifying 1-hour runtime on a 1500 mAh LiPo battery.
LED Status Panel3D-printed handle insert housing the power button recess and dual indicator LEDs — green signals the klister has reached the target 35–40°C application temperature, yellow signals warming.
Full Prototype — Final AssemblyTop-down view of the fully assembled KlisterFixer printed in purple PLA, showing the complete form factor: klister tube chamber, sliding cover, handle, and trigger arm. This was the primary prototype used for field testing and the demo day presentation.
Earlier Iteration — Housing GeometryA mid-development prototype in light blue showing the evolving tube-housing geometry and trigger mechanism. Iteration between this version and the final design refined trigger wall thickness, cover sliding clearance, and LED slot placement.
Mechanical Components — Disassembled3D-printed gray housing components laid out: main tube chamber, sliding cover, and trigger arm with pivot pin, exposing the internal channel through which the klister tube is loaded and heated.
Trigger & Pin — Printed PartsFreshly printed yellow PLA trigger arm and retaining pin — the core mechanical actuators that expel klister from the tube when squeezed, designed to be operable with standard ski gloves.
Electronics IntegrationAssembled prototype combining the LiPo battery pack with the 3D-printed housing and wired electronics module, evaluating fit, weight distribution, and grip ergonomics before final sealing.
Prototype Fit CheckVerifying integration of battery, housing, and circuit module while evaluating overall balance and grip in hand — key feedback loop between electronics and mechanical subteams during final assembly.
Build Session — Prototype AssemblyTeam member inspecting the assembled KlisterFixer prototype during a lab session, integrating subcomponents and checking mechanical clearances before electronics are enclosed.
Design Iteration SketchesHand-drawn engineering sketch showing side and top views with key dimensions alongside a 'Future Fixes' list — including a hole for the klister tube tip, rounded handle, thicker trigger walls, and improved slider cover geometry. These sketches directly drove the next prototype revision.
Field Test — Glove-Compatible UseOutdoor validation with the CU Boulder Nordic Ski Club: hands remain clean throughout the entire application process, confirming the core design requirement of zero direct klister contact while wearing standard ski gloves in cold conditions.
Application Result — Even CoverageKlister applied evenly along the ski base using the KlisterFixer, demonstrating consistent wax distribution. Traditional hand-application takes 15–30 minutes and leaves hands coated in wax; the device achieves comparable coverage in under 10 minutes.
Electronics & Systems Integration

Designed and built for CU Boulder's GEEN 2400 Engineering Projects for the Community course, in partnership with the CU Nordic Ski Club. Klister wax is critical for cross-country ski grip in icy conditions, but applying it is slow, messy, and nearly impossible outdoors in cold weather with gloves. The KlisterFixer Applicator is a handheld, battery-powered device that heats klister to 35–40°C within 10 minutes, expels it via a squeeze trigger, and signals optimal temperature through LED indicators — all without direct hand contact with the wax.

  • Designed and iterated the temperature-sensing indicator circuit using an Arduino Nano, transistor-switched resistive heating element, and dual LED feedback (green = ready, yellow = warming) to signal when optimal klister application temperature was reached.
  • Bench-validated heating element performance with a Keysight programmable power supply and Agilent multimeter, confirming 11.3V / 1.13A / 12.8W operating point and sizing a 1500 mAh LiPo battery for 1-hour field use.
  • Contributed to the iterative design process through engineering sketches, design reviews, and prototype feedback — proposing improvements to trigger geometry, LED slot placement, and housing ergonomics across multiple printed revisions.
  • Supported prototype assembly, integrating LiPo battery, PCB, and wiring harness into the 3D-printed housing and foam-backed grip module across successive iterations.
  • Participated in outdoor field trials with the Nordic Ski Club, validating glove-compatible usability and confirming even wax coverage — reducing application time from 15–30 minutes to under 10 minutes per person.
